title: The Wild Robot
commentary: {"overall": "The Wild Robot is a structurally rich film built around a genuinely ordinary (if artificial) protagonist who earns every relationship and survival skill through effort, failure, and adaptation. title: Kiki's Delivery Service
commentary: {"overall": "Kiki's Delivery Service is a quietly radical film about a young girl who builds a small business from nothing in a strange city, and whose central crisis is creative burnout and loss of identity rather than any external villain. title: The Fox and the Hound

title: Flow

title: Paddington 2

title: Howl's Moving Castle

title: The Nightmare Before Christmas
commentary: {"overall": "Jack Skellington is the self-made king of Halloween Town — a role earned by talent and persona, not birth — but the story's real engine is his existential crisis of meaning and identity, which gives it genuine emotional depth. title: Strange World

title: 101 Dalmatians

title: The Prince of Egypt
commentary: {"overall": "The Prince of Egypt is a theologically serious epic about identity, calling, and liberation — Moses is born into royal privilege but the story actively frames that privilege as a gilded cage and ultimately a lie, while his divine calling introduces a destiny-assisted dimension that keeps agency from being fully self-generated. title: The Mighty Ducks

title: Home on the Range
commentary: {"overall": "Home on the Range is a modest, largely forgotten Disney western in which three dairy cows try to save their farm by capturing an outlaw for the bounty money — a livelihood goal rather than a status goal. title: Treasure Planet
commentary: {"overall": "Treasure Planet is a sci-fi retelling of Treasure Island centered on a troubled, ordinary boy whose real journey is about father-wound healing and self-worth rather than treasure acquisition — the treasure is ultimately rejected in favor of earned belonging. title: Atlantis: The Lost Empire

title: The Croods

title: How to Train Your Dragon 2
commentary: {"overall": "HTTYD2 is a structurally rich sequel anchored in genuine emotional weight — grief, identity, and the burden of leadership — but Hiccup's specialness is substantially born-into: he is the chief's son, destined to lead, and the story's resolution literally installs him as chief. title: Shrek 2
commentary: {"overall": "Shrek 2 is a fairy-tale parody that actively critiques royal status and the 'happily ever after' fantasy, but its plot mechanics still revolve heavily around the kingdom of Far Far Away and the inherited power of the Fairy Godmother and Prince Charming — born-into greatness is the antagonist force but also the structural engine. the story critiques this but the critique is incomplete because the resolution still involves operating within that royal world rather than escaping it, landing it in the 16-20 band rather than the critique-as-hollow 30 band.", "agency": "Shrek drives most of his own choices and the climax is largely his doing, but the resolution is substantially assembled by Fairy Godmother's potion mechanics, Puss and Donkey's assistance, and a deus-ex-machina kiss reversal — strong but meaningfully split, fitting the 13 band.", "core": "The film has real emotional substance — Shrek's fear that he is not enough for Fiona and her world, and Fiona's rejection of the 'beautiful' ideal — but it is delivered with enough comedic deflection that it sits at the simpler-arc 13 rather than the deep-honest-core 17.", "music": "Features strong licensed songs used diegetically and memorably (Holding Out for a Hero, I Need a Hero staging; Accidentally in Love) plus some original score work, but no original story-carrying songs in the Frozen/Moana sense — fits the 8 band.", "inclusion": "Band 10/10 (inclusion/anti-prejudice IS the lesson): The entire film, like the first, is structured around the thesis that outsiders, the ugly, and the 'wrong' kind of person deserve love and belonging — Shrek, Fiona, Donkey, and Puss are all othered by the royal establishment, and the story's moral engine is explicitly anti-prejudice and anti-conformity.", "romance": "Romance is present but the film actively subverts the 'being chosen by the handsome prince' fantasy — Fiona rejects Prince Charming and the beautiful-human ideal, and the ending endorses the unconventional relationship over the socially approved one, earning the full 6."}

title: The Lego Batman Movie
commentary: {"overall": "The Lego Batman Movie is a surprisingly deep film about a hyper-wealthy, born-special superhero who must learn that family and vulnerability matter more than lone-wolf status — the emotional core is genuinely honest and the story actively subverts the 'cool billionaire' fantasy. Batman's inherited wealth and born-into-greatness are present but the narrative explicitly critiques them as hollow, landing him in the royal-but-burdened band rather than the unexamined-greatness band. The climax is shared and destiny-assisted enough to dilute full agency.", "wealth": "Batman is a billionaire superhero whose status is the source of his specialness, but the entire film's thesis is that this status is a defense mechanism and a trap — closer to 'royal-but-burdened / status as isolation' (band 16) than unexamined greatness, though the critique is thorough enough to flirt with the 28-30 range; torn between bands, picking the lower one because his wealth and born-specialness still do significant narrative work.", "agency": "Batman drives most of his own story and makes the key emotional choice to accept family, but the climax relies heavily on ensemble cooperation, Robin's contributions, and a shared solution — strong but split, landing at 13.", "core": "The film's emotional core is genuinely about fear of intimacy, grief over lost family, and the courage to be vulnerable — honest and specific enough to earn the top band alongside Inside Out and Frozen.", "music": "The film features original comedic songs (Batman's self-aggrandizing metal tracks, 'Friends Are Family') that carry character and theme, but they are more gag-driven than narratively load-bearing in the way Moana or Frozen songs are — strong songs with gaps earns an 8.", "inclusion": "Band 8/10 (strong inclusion content, secondary to main arc): The film builds a found-family across wildly different characters, includes Barbara Gordon as a competent co-lead, and adopts a diverse surrogate son in Robin, with inclusion themes present but subordinate to the emotional arc about loneliness.", "romance": "Romance is entirely absent as a prize or plot driver — Batman's arc is about chosen family, not romantic partnership, earning the full 6."}

title: Puss in Boots: The Last Wish

title: Meet the Robinsons

title: Pinocchio

The emotional core around honesty, temptation, and belonging is genuine if somewhat didactic, and the songs are among the most story-integrated in Disney's canon.", "wealth": "Geppetto is a humble craftsman with no status, and the story's reward is personhood/belonging rather than wealth or status ascension — but the transformation is gifted by the Blue Fairy rather than earned through a depicted grind, placing it in the Willy Wonka range: a poor honest protagonist whose prize is still externally bestowed, scoring around 15.", "agency": "Pinocchio makes choices throughout but is repeatedly rescued — Jiminy Cricket guides him, the Blue Fairy intervenes multiple times, and the climactic escape from Monstro is self-initiated but luck-heavy; this is a shared/luck-assisted solve rather than a clean protagonist victory.", "core": "The story carries real substance around honesty, temptation, and the desire to belong and be 'real,' but the arc is episodic and the emotional beats are more illustrative than deeply felt — real substance, simpler arc.", "music": "Songs like 'When You Wish Upon a Star,' 'Give a Little Whistle,' and 'I've Got No Strings' are original, iconic, and directly carry character and theme — full marks.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive Exclusion): The world is a homogeneous European village presented as default; no harmful stereotyping rises to active harm, but there is a missed opportunity and passive exclusion throughout.", "romance": "Romance is entirely absent from the narrative; the central relationship is paternal, scoring full marks."}

title: Lady and the Tramp
commentary: {"overall": "Lady and the Tramp is a gentle romance whose structural reward is explicitly being-chosen and domestication; Lady's arc ends with Tramp absorbed into bourgeois household comfort, and the story's emotional substance is real but modest. The film is significantly damaged by the Siamese cat sequence, which encodes harmful ethnic stereotyping as spectacle.", "wealth": "Lady belongs to a comfortable middle-class household from birth and the story's endorsed endpoint is Tramp joining that domestic comfort — status is unexamined and the reward is settling into an established home, landing near the Cars band (20) but slightly higher because Tramp does undergo a genuine character change rather than pure inheritance.", "agency": "Lady and Tramp both make meaningful choices — she escapes the pound, he rescues the baby — but the climax (Tramp killing the rat) is the key act of agency and it is genuinely his own; however Lady spends much of the middle act being acted upon (muzzled, impounded, rescued), splitting the agency enough to fall below the strong band.", "core": "The film has real emotional substance about belonging, freedom vs. domesticity, and what it means to be loved and trusted — Tramp's arc from stray to family member carries genuine feeling — but it is simpler and less layered than the top band.", "music": "The film features original songs ('Bella Notte,' 'He's a Tramp,' 'The Siamese Cat Song') that do carry story and character, but the catalogue is thin and uneven, placing it in the strong-with-gaps band.", "inclusion": "Band 2/10 (Harmful stereotyping present in the text): The Siamese cat sequence ('The Siamese Cat Song') uses exaggerated Asian vocal caricature and visual coding to represent scheming, destructive foreigners — this is textbook harmful ethnic othering embedded in the narrative, not merely passive absence.", "romance": "Being chosen, domesticated, and coupled is explicitly the structural reward for both protagonists — Tramp's entire arc resolves in marriage and puppies, and Lady's happiness is defined by romantic partnership, placing this squarely at 0."}

title: Wish
commentary: {"overall": "Wish is a Disney centennial celebration built around a royal-adjacent protagonist whose specialness is partly innate (she alone can see Star, she alone resists Magnifico) and whose emotional arc is thin — more about defeating a villain than excavating a genuine internal wound. The structure endorses community solidarity over individual status-climbing, which keeps it out of the lowest bands, but the story never earns the depth it gestures toward.", "wealth": "Asha is a commoner but her specialness is narratively innate — she uniquely perceives Star and is uniquely resistant to Magnifico's corruption — placing her in the royal-but-burdened / born-into-specialness band (16-20 range); the kingdom setting does narrative work but the reward is liberation of the community rather than status ascension, so 16 rather than lower.", "agency": "Asha drives the central plan and makes the key choices, but the literal magical agent (Star) does much of the heavy lifting in the climax, and the community rallies to assist; this is strong-but-split, landing at 13.", "core": "The film gestures at themes of dreams, hope, and institutional power corrupting good intentions, but these remain surface-level; the emotional core never achieves the honest interiority of grief or identity that earns 17, settling into standard adventure/thematic beats closer to 8.", "music": "Original songs by Julia Michaels are present and attempt to carry story beats ('This Wish,' 'At All Costs'), but they are broadly considered weak and forgettable compared to Disney's best; strong songs with notable gaps earns 8.", "inclusion": "Band 7/10 (Meaningful representation, not the lesson): The kingdom of Rosas is visually diverse with a Mediterranean/Spanish-influenced setting and a multicultural ensemble cast, and this representation feels intentional, but anti-prejudice is not the film's lesson.", "romance": "A romantic subplot with Asha and Hal exists but is entirely peripheral and never framed as the prize or resolution; the story ends on community triumph, not romantic union, earning a 6."}

title: The Black Cauldron
commentary: {"overall": "The Black Cauldron is a dark, earnest fantasy adventure that largely fails to cohere emotionally or structurally. Taran is a farm-boy assistant pig-keeper with modest aspirations to heroism, but the story repeatedly rescues him through external agents — the cauldron is ultimately neutralized by Gurgi's self-sacrifice, not Taran's choices — and the emotional core never deepens beyond surface-level adventure beats.", "wealth": "Taran is a commoner with no inherited status or wealth; his goal is to prove himself as a warrior, not to ascend socially, placing him near the 20-band but slightly above because his ordinariness is genuine and the story does not endorse status as a reward — he ends up back where he started, which is closer to the 20-25 range than the Cars band.", "agency": "Taran makes choices and drives the quest, but the climax is resolved by Gurgi's sacrifice and the witches' intervention, not by Taran's own decisive action — this is a split/luck-assisted resolution landing in the 8-13 range; torn between the two, the lower band applies because the key cauldron-destruction moment is not his.", "core": "The story gestures at themes of humility, true heroism versus glory-seeking, and selfless sacrifice (Gurgi), but these are underdeveloped and delivered through plot mechanics rather than genuine emotional excavation — standard adventure beats with a thin thematic veneer.", "music": "There are no memorable original songs; the film relies almost entirely on Elmer Bernstein's orchestral score with no diegetic or narrative-carrying songs, placing it below even the score-only band of 5 due to the score's limited emotional impact.", "inclusion": "Band 5/10 (Neutral): The cast is a generic fantasy ensemble with no real-world group representation, no prejudice lesson, and no active harm — a neutral non-human-adjacent fantasy world that neither teaches inclusion nor causes harm.", "romance": "A mild romantic thread between Taran and Eilonwy is present but entirely incidental and unresolved — it is not the prize or the point of the story, but it is present enough to preclude a 6; it sits closer to a weak 3 but the imbalance toward adventure over romance keeps it from a full 3."}

title: The Santa Clause
commentary: {"overall": "The Santa Clause is a fantasy comedy in which an ordinary divorced dad accidentally inherits the most powerful gift-giving role in the world by contractual obligation — the ultimate unearned ascension. The emotional core about a father reconnecting with his son is genuine and warm, but the structural reward is literally inheriting a magical empire, wealth, and global status through a clause in a contract rather than any earned merit.", "wealth": "Scott Calvin does nothing to earn Santa's role — he falls into it via a legal loophole after a man dies on his roof; the endorsed reward is inheriting the North Pole operation, magical powers, and global status, placing this firmly near the 'status ascension as endorsed reward' end of the spectrum, closer to Willy Wonka territory (a lucky ordinary person who inherits a magical enterprise).", "agency": "Scott makes choices — he resists, then accepts, then fights to keep his son's belief — but the transformation is largely destiny-driven and compelled by the Clause itself rather than freely chosen striving, landing in the strong-but-destiny-assisted band.", "core": "The real story underneath is a divorced father proving his love and rebuilding his relationship with his son Charlie, which is a genuine and emotionally resonant arc, though it is simpler and more sitcom-adjacent than the deepest examples in this band.", "music": "The film uses a score and a handful of incidental holiday songs but has no original narrative-carrying songs; score-only band applies.", "inclusion": "Band 5/10 (Neutral): The cast is largely homogeneous and suburban; no group is harmed and no inclusion lesson is taught — a neutral non-event.", "romance": "Romance is entirely absent from the narrative structure; Scott's ex-wife is a source of conflict, not a prize, and no romantic reward is endorsed."}

title: The Lorax
commentary: {"overall": "The Lorax is a film where the protagonist Ted matters primarily because he is the chosen boy who will restore the world, and the villain O'Hare has built an entire economy around artificial air — status and wealth ascension are central to the antagonist's arc and the story's reward structure leans toward Ted winning Audrey's affection and the town's adulation. The environmental message is real but the narrative structure undermines it by making Ted's motivation initially romantic and his success feel destiny-adjacent rather than earned through genuine grind.", "wealth": "O'Hare's monopoly on bottled air is explicitly critiqued as hollow and corrupt, but Ted's reward includes social elevation and romantic payoff in a town that celebrates him — the story does not fully escape endorsing a status-ascension outcome, landing it near the 0-16 band rather than the 30+ critique band.", "agency": "Ted makes active choices and drives the plot forward, but the Once-ler's backstory does much of the thematic heavy lifting and the Lorax himself frames the moral stakes — agency is strong but meaningfully split across characters.", "core": "The environmental stewardship message is genuine and the Once-ler's arc carries real regret and consequence, but the emotional core is simpler and more didactic than deeply personal, landing closer to the 13 band than 17.", "music": "Original songs are present and do narrative work (e.g., 'How Bad Can I Be?' effectively characterizes the Once-ler's moral decline) but several numbers feel more like spectacle than story-carriers, placing this in the strong-with-gaps band.", "inclusion": "Band 5/10 (Neutral): The cast is largely non-human or a generic small-town ensemble with no harmful stereotyping but no meaningful inclusion lesson either — purely neutral.", "romance": "Audrey is Ted's motivation but she is a character with her own passion (trees), and the romance is present but secondary to the environmental plot — balanced rather than the prize."}

title: Robin Hood
commentary: {"overall": "The 1973 Disney animated Robin Hood is fundamentally a story about a folk hero whose entire identity is tied to noble birth (he is literally a fox of aristocratic lineage reclaiming a world of lords and kings), and the endorsed reward is restoring the monarchy and marrying Maid Marian — a princess. The emotional core has genuine warmth around justice for the poor, but the structural logic is deeply status-driven.", "wealth": "Robin is a nobleman-outlaw whose heroism is inseparable from his aristocratic identity, and the climax restores royal order under King Richard — status ascension and monarchical legitimacy are the endorsed reward, placing this firmly near the bottom of the scale; scored slightly above Cinderella/Aladdin because Robin does perform active deeds rather than simply being elevated by birth alone.", "agency": "Robin drives most of his own plans — the archery tournament gambit, the jailbreak — but the final resolution depends heavily on King Richard's timely return to impose justice, splitting the solving between Robin's agency and deus-ex-monarch rescue.", "core": "There is a genuine theme of justice for the oppressed poor versus corrupt authority, which gives the film real substance, but it is relatively simple and never deepens into identity or grief territory.", "music": "Roger Miller's folk-country songs ('Oo-de-lally', 'Not in Nottingham') carry mood and narrative efficiently but are few in number and leave large stretches of score-only storytelling.", "inclusion": "Band 5/10 (Neutral): The all-animal cast sidesteps human demographic representation entirely; no group is harmed or stereotyped in a meaningful way, but no inclusion lesson is taught either.", "romance": "Maid Marian is a prize and the marriage at the end is a celebrated reward, but Robin's primary motivation throughout is justice for the poor rather than winning her hand, making it present-but-balanced rather than the sole prize."}

title: Oliver & Company
commentary: {"overall": "Oliver & Company is a loose Dickens adaptation where a stray kitten's journey ends with him being adopted into a wealthy family — status ascension via adoption into wealth is the explicit endorsed reward, unexamined and unearned. The emotional core has some genuine belonging/loneliness beats but they are thin, and the gang of street dogs is ultimately discarded as a lifestyle rather than celebrated. The film sits firmly in the low-wealth band because the narrative resolution is Oliver leaving poverty for a rich girl's mansion, presented as unambiguously good.", "wealth": "The story's endorsed reward is Oliver being permanently adopted into Jenny's wealthy household — ascension into wealth and status is the climax's payoff, placing this near the Cinderella/Aladdin band; scored slightly above 0 because Oliver is a kitten (not a person seeking status) and the framing is 'love and belonging' rather than pure status hunger, but the structural outcome is identical.", "agency": "Oliver makes some choices (returning to Fagin's gang, then choosing Jenny) but the climax is largely resolved by Dodger and the gang rescuing him and Jenny, and Sykes is defeated by an external accident (train); agency is split and luck-assisted, landing between the strong and luck-heavy bands.", "core": "There is a real loneliness/belonging thread — Oliver as an unwanted orphan seeking a home — but it is handled superficially and resolved by wealth rather than earned connection, keeping it below the 'deep honest core' band.", "music": "The film features several original songs ('Why Should I Worry,' 'Perfect Isn't Easy,' 'Streets of Gold') that carry character and mood, but the songwriting is uneven and not all songs advance the story meaningfully, placing it in the strong-with-gaps band.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive exclusion / homogeneous world as default): The villain Sykes and his henchmen lean on crime-underworld stereotyping, and the world is largely presented as a default New York without meaningful representation or anti-prejudice content; no active degradation but no inclusion lesson either.", "romance": "Romance is entirely absent from the narrative; the central relationship is a child-and-pet bond, scoring the maximum."}

title: The Parent Trap
commentary: {"overall": "The 1998 Parent Trap is a charming but structurally wealth-saturated film: the twins are born into considerable privilege (Napa Valley vineyard, London fashion world), and the story's resolution rewards them with reunited wealthy parents and the defeat of a gold-digging social climber — status is both the backdrop and the prize. The emotional core around family reunion is genuine but the narrative is firmly anchored in an upper-class world presented as aspirational and unexamined.", "wealth": "The twins are born into significant inherited wealth (vineyard estate, fashion empire) that does substantial narrative work — the setting, the stakes, and the villain's motivation all hinge on this status, which is never critiqued; this lands well below the 20-band, closer to the 'born-into greatness, unexamined' territory with status ascension (restoring the wealthy family unit) as the endorsed reward.", "agency": "The twins are the architects of the reunion scheme and execute it with real ingenuity, but the ultimate resolution depends heavily on the parents' adult choices and the convenient collapse of the antagonist, splitting credit enough to land in the strong-but-diluted band.", "core": "There is real emotional substance in the twins' longing for the missing parent and the ache of a fractured family, but it is delivered with a light comic touch that keeps it from reaching the deeper grief or identity excavation of the top band.", "music": "The film uses a licensed/score-based soundtrack with some diegetic music moments (the camp guitar scene) but no original story-carrying songs, placing it firmly in the score/licensed band.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive exclusion): The world of the film is almost entirely white and wealthy, presented as default and aspirational with no meaningful representation or lesson; the villain is coded as a scheming social climber in ways that reinforce rather than challenge class prejudice.", "romance": "Parental romance is central to the plot's resolution but is balanced against the children's family-reunion goal rather than being framed as the prize for the protagonist, landing in the middle band."}

title: The Emperor's New Groove

title: Casper
commentary: {"overall": "Casper is a film where the central reward is literally inheriting a vast haunted mansion — Whipstaff Manor is the explicit prize Carrigan pursues and which Kat's family occupies — and Casper's specialness is entirely a function of his ghostly birth-state, not anything he earns. The emotional core around grief and belonging is genuine and touching, but the structural scaffolding is built on inherited wealth and status as the story's engine.", "wealth": "The mansion is the story's MacGuffin and ultimate prize; Kat's father is hired specifically to access it, and Casper's identity is inseparable from his born-into ghostly aristocracy in that estate — this is closer to the 'unexamined born-into greatness' band (20) but the mansion-as-status-reward and Casper's passive specialness push it down toward the 0–16 range; scored 10 because status ascension (occupying the manor) is the structural reward, though it is not quite as nakedly endorsed as Cinderella.", "agency": "Kat and Casper both take meaningful actions at the climax, but the resolution depends heavily on a convenient wish-granting mechanism and adult intervention (Dr. Harvey's ghost, the Lazarus machine), placing this in the luck-heavy/shared-solving band.", "core": "There is real emotional substance — Casper's grief over his lost childhood and Kat's grief over her dead mother give the film genuine heart — but it is simpler and less rigorously developed than the top-tier examples, landing in the 'real substance, simpler arc' band.", "music": "The film relies primarily on James Horner's orchestral score with no original story-carrying songs; the 'Remember Me This Way' end-credits song is peripheral to the narrative.", "inclusion": "Band 5/10 (Neutral): The cast is largely homogeneous and inclusion is neither taught nor harmed; non-human characters (ghosts) dominate the supernatural side, making this a passive-neutral entry.", "romance": "A tentative proto-romance between Casper and Kat is present and sweet but is clearly secondary and unresolved — not a prize-as-marriage structure, landing in the balanced middle band."}

title: Anastasia
commentary: {"overall": "Anastasia is structurally a lost-princess story whose entire engine is the revelation and restoration of royal identity — the endorsed reward is literally reclaiming aristocratic birthright and marrying a handsome con-man-turned-prince-consort. The emotional core about memory and belonging has genuine warmth, but it is ultimately in service of status restoration rather than independent of it.", "wealth": "The story's climax and reward is Anastasia reclaiming her identity as a Romanov princess and choosing a life of wealth and status; even though she 'chooses love over the crown,' she still ends up with both — this is squarely in the 0–5 band alongside Cinderella and Aladdin where status ascension (or restoration) is the endorsed outcome.", "agency": "Anastasia makes meaningful choices — she chooses to go to Paris, she confronts Rasputin at the climax herself, and she ultimately chooses her own path over the Empress's wishes — but Dimitri's intervention and the magical/supernatural elements dilute her sole ownership of the resolution, placing her in the strong-but-split band.", "core": "There is a real emotional arc about identity, memory, and belonging — 'who am I and where do I come from?' — that gives the film genuine substance beyond pure adventure, though it is somewhat undermined by the romance and status-restoration framing.", "music": "The score features original songs ('Journey to the Past,' 'Once Upon a December,' 'In the Dark of the Night') that directly carry character motivation and story momentum, earning the top band.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive Exclusion): The film presents an almost entirely white European aristocratic world as default with no meaningful representation or inclusion lesson; the villain is coded as supernaturally 'other' but this does not rise to active harmful stereotyping — it is passive homogeneity and missed opportunity.", "romance": "Being chosen by and ending up with Dimitri — sailing away together — is explicitly the film's final image and endorsed reward, making romance/being-chosen the prize in the Cinderella/Little Mermaid band."}

title: Mufasa: The Lion King
commentary: {"overall": "Mufasa is a prequel origin story that traces how an ordinary orphaned lion cub rises to become king, but the narrative ultimately endorses royal bloodline destiny — Mufasa is revealed to be 'chosen' and ascends to kingship, making inherited/ascended status the structural reward. Agency is meaningful but destiny-assisted, and the emotional core around brotherhood and belonging is real but somewhat diluted by the franchise mythology scaffolding.", "wealth": "Despite starting as a nobody orphan, the story's entire arc is engineered to explain how Mufasa becomes the legendary king, making royal status ascension the explicit endorsed reward — this lands firmly in the 0–10 band alongside The Lion King (4), as the climax is about claiming a throne and legacy, not a livelihood.", "agency": "Mufasa makes meaningful choices and fights his own battles, but the narrative repeatedly frames him as destined and chosen by Aselé/the Great Kings, diluting pure agency into destiny-assisted territory — strong but not fully self-determined, placing it between the 8 and 13 bands; torn, so scored 10.", "core": "There is genuine emotional substance in the brotherhood between Mufasa and Taka/Scar — themes of jealousy, belonging, and the corruption of resentment give the film real heart — but it is simpler and more franchise-obligated than a fully resonant arc, fitting the 13 band.", "music": "Lin-Manuel Miranda contributes original songs that do carry story beats and character emotion, but they are uneven in integration and don't reach the wall-to-wall narrative function of Moana or Frozen — strong songs with gaps places this at 8.", "inclusion": "Band 3/10 (present but balanced): The film features a diverse voice cast and African-inspired setting with meaningful representation, but like its predecessor it presents a monarchical animal world where othering of the villain-coded lion (scarred, darker) is structurally embedded in the text, and no anti-prejudice lesson is taught — harmful othering is present but not spectacle-level, pulling it below neutral.", "romance": "Romance between Mufasa and Sarabi is present and narratively meaningful but is clearly secondary to the brotherhood/destiny arc — it is not the prize, placing it in the balanced middle band at 3."}

title: The Aristocats
commentary: {"overall": "The Aristocats is a film whose entire premise is restoring a family of pampered, wealthy cats to their inherited aristocratic lifestyle — the reward is literally returning to inherited status and wealth, unexamined and fully endorsed. title: The Sword in the Stone
commentary: {"overall": "The Sword in the Stone is a textbook case of born-into-greatness narrative: Wart/Arthur matters entirely because he is the secret heir to the throne of England, and the entire story exists to deliver him to that destiny. His agency is real but heavily destiny-assisted, and the emotional core is a pleasant but shallow 'education and potential' arc rather than anything deeply felt.", "wealth": "The endorsed reward is literally ascending to the throne of England — the ultimate status ascension — and Arthur's specialness is entirely a function of his secret royal blood, placing this squarely in the 0-band alongside The Lion King and Cinderella.", "agency": "Arthur does make choices and participates actively in his own education, but the climax is resolved by a magical destiny (only the true-born king can pull the sword) rather than earned skill or decision, splitting the score toward the luck/destiny-assisted band.", "core": "The story gestures at themes of knowledge, potential, and believing in oneself, but these are standard adventure/education beats rather than a deeply honest emotional core; the real engine is the destiny reveal.", "music": "The film features original Sherman Brothers songs ('Higitus Figitus,' 'That's What Makes the World Go Round,' 'Mad Madam Mim') that are charming and diegetically integrated but not consistently story-driving across the whole film.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive Exclusion): The world is a homogeneous medieval England presented as default with no meaningful representation or anti-prejudice content; no active harm but a clear missed opportunity.", "romance": "Romance is entirely absent from the narrative — Wart has no love interest and being-chosen refers only to kingship, not a romantic partner — scoring at the top of this dimension."}

title: The Road to El Dorado
commentary: {"overall": "Two con-men stumble into a lost city of gold and are mistaken for gods, with the entire narrative engine being the acquisition of enormous wealth and status; the story endorses the treasure haul as the reward even as the protagonists ultimately sacrifice the gold, but the climax is still framed around escaping with their lives and a modest share of riches. The emotional core is a bromance with some real warmth but the structure is fundamentally a caper. The film's depiction of the indigenous Mesoamerican population as credulous, superstitious, and easily manipulated by two European tricksters is a meaningful textual problem.", "wealth": "Status-ascension-as-goal dominates the entire plot — the protagonists pursue a literal city of gold and are worshipped as gods — placing this firmly in the 0-band even though they partially renounce the treasure at the end; the endorsed pursuit and the god-status reward are never seriously critiqued.", "agency": "Tulio and Miguel drive most of their own choices and schemes, but Chel's assistance, Altivo the horse, and a fair amount of convenient luck dilute their agency enough to land in the split/destiny-assisted band rather than a clean 17.", "core": "There is a genuine friendship-vs-ambition tension at the heart of the film and the bromance pays off emotionally, but it never reaches the depth of grief, identity, or fear-vs-love that characterises the 17-band; it sits at standard adventure/friendship beats with a slightly warmer execution.", "music": "Elton John's songs ('El Dorado,' 'Without Question,' 'Friends Never Say Goodbye') carry emotional beats and are memorable, but several key story moments are scored rather than sung, and the songs are not as tightly integrated into the narrative structure as Moana or Frozen, placing this in the strong-with-gaps band.", "inclusion": "Band 2/10 (Harmful stereotyping present in the text): The entire indigenous population of El Dorado is portrayed as naive, superstitious, and easily deceived by two European grifters who are worshipped as gods — this is a textual othering of a Mesoamerican people that goes beyond passive exclusion into active harmful framing, even if played for comedy.", "romance": "Chel's romance with Tulio is present and given real screen time but is clearly secondary to the male friendship arc and does not function as the prize or resolution of the story, landing in the balanced middle band."}

title: The Swan Princess
commentary: {"overall": "The Swan Princess is a royal-birth-to-royal-marriage pipeline with almost no structural deviation: Odette is a princess whose entire story is about being cursed until a prince can break the spell, and the endorsed reward is the royal marriage. The emotional core is thin — the film gestures at Odette wanting to be valued for more than her looks, but the structure immediately contradicts this by having her rescued and married. Agency is nearly absent at the climax.", "wealth": "Status ascension is the endorsed reward from birth to marriage — both protagonists are royals and the story ends with their union cementing that status; this lands squarely in the 0–5 band alongside Cinderella and Aladdin.", "agency": "Odette is transformed into a swan and spends most of the film waiting; at the climax Derek fights Rothbart and Odette dies and is revived by his vow — she does not solve her own story, placing her near the passive end, slightly above Snow White only because she verbally refuses Rothbart's deal.", "core": "The film briefly raises the idea that Odette wants to be loved for who she is rather than her beauty, but this theme is never developed with honesty or depth — it is stated once and then abandoned in favor of standard adventure/rescue beats.", "music": "The film has several original songs ('Far Longer Than Forever,' 'This Is My Idea,' 'No More Mr. Nice Guy') that carry character and story beats, earning a strong-songs score, though the songwriting quality is uneven and some numbers feel perfunctory.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive Exclusion): The cast is a homogeneous European fairy-tale world presented as default with no meaningful representation or anti-prejudice content; no active harm but a clear missed opportunity.", "romance": "Marriage to the prince is explicitly and unambiguously the entire prize of the narrative — Odette's freedom, identity, and survival are all resolved through Derek's romantic vow and their wedding; this is a textbook 0."}

title: Thumbelina
commentary: {"overall": "The 1994 Don Bluth Thumbelina is a near-textbook case of the lowest-scoring profile: a tiny girl whose entire story is about being found, chosen, and delivered to a fairy prince who is her destined match, with virtually no self-determination at any climax. Her specialness is innate (she is literally born magical and fairy-sized), the reward is royal marriage, and the emotional core never rises above 'be chosen by the right man.'", "wealth": "Scores in the 0-band: Thumbelina's specialness is entirely innate (born magically from a flower, destined for fairy royalty), and the endorsed reward is literal royal ascension through marriage to Prince Cornelius — status ascension is the explicit prize.", "agency": "Scores near 0: Thumbelina is kidnapped, traded, and transported by a succession of antagonists and helpers; at the climax it is Cornelius and the swallow Jacquimo who rescue her — she does not solve her own story and is largely passive throughout.", "core": "The emotional core is almost purely 'being chosen by the right person' and longing for romantic validation, with no meaningful exploration of identity, grief, or belonging beyond 'I want to find where I fit' — which is resolved entirely by royal marriage rather than self-discovery.", "music": "Barry Manilow's original songs ('Let Me Be Your Wings,' 'Soon') are melodically prominent and do carry plot and longing, but the songwriting is uneven and several numbers feel like filler, placing this in the strong-songs-with-gaps band.", "inclusion": "Band 4/10 (Passive Exclusion): The world is homogeneous and the 'othering' of Thumbelina by toads, moles, and beetles is played for comic villainy rather than as an anti-prejudice lesson; no group is actively degraded but no inclusion lesson is taught either.", "romance": "Scores 0: being chosen by and married to a fairy prince IS the entire prize and the resolution of every conflict in the film — the most direct possible example of marriage-as-reward framing."}
